title: Add more test coverage to engine new payload (osaka)

body:
## PR Description

Engine Flow Test: Prague → Osaka Transition

Step | Method | Description / Expected Result
-- | -- | --
1 | get_genesis_block_info | Initialize test at Prague fork
2 | sendRawTransaction | Send Blob Tx V0 → Accepted
3 | sendRawTransaction | Send Blob Tx V1 → Rejected ("blobs failed kzg validation")
4 | getBlobsV1 | Returns data from Step 2
5 | getBlobsV2 | Fails → "Fork not supported" (since V2 is Osaka+)
6 | forkchoiceUpdatedV3 | Start preparing payloads (Prague time)
7 | getPayloadV4 | Returns payload with blob from Step 2
8 | getPayloadV5 | Fails → "Osaka not enabled"
9 | forkchoiceUpdatedV3 | Switch to Osaka time, start new payloads
10 | getPayloadV4 | Fails (Prague fork expired)
11 | getPayloadV5 | Returns payload with blob from Step 2
12 | newPayloadV4 | Send new payload with blob from Step 2 → Accepted
13 | sendRawTransaction | Send another Blob Tx V0 → Accepted
14 | forkchoiceUpdatedV3 | Finalize fork choice, confirm Osaka
15 | getBlobsV1 | Fails → "Fork not supported" (since V1 is Cancun+ and Osaka-)
16 | getBlobsV2 | Query blob from Step 13 → Returns null (Tx was V0, no conversion)
17 | sendRawTransaction | Send Blob Tx V1 → Accepted (Osaka is live)
18 | getBlobsV2 | Returns blob data from Step 17
19 | forkchoiceUpdatedV3 | Prepare payload including blob from Step 17
20 | getPayloadV5 | Returns payload with blob from Step 17

</body></html>
title: test: add Osaka Engine acceptance test

body:
There appear to be some redundant conversions in the input processing e.g. clampedToInt(clampedToLong()) in `BigIntegerModularExponentiationPrecompiledContract`.

The long values are used for gas calculation but maybe we can use ints due to https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-7823#analysis
This may only be possible post-osaka; maybe also possible for pre-osaka mainnet based on the usage analysis (assuming we don't get some txs with > int values executed in the meantime).
Perhaps on other networks we would still need to support longs for the inputs though.

---

Regardless, we can split these input processing/gas calculation codepaths if it proves to be a worthwhile optimisation.

body:
## PR description
This PR adds several missing ModExp test cases as well as enhances the `BenchmarkSubCommand` in multiple ways. I just did the ModExp benchmark not to make this PR too big but will be migrating all precompile test cases from `Benchmarks.java` to evmtool and reconcile the output. New suggested output for Precompiles going forward:

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/64797f57-3ea7-4642-a70f-94353061cbfb)

Note that these benchmarks don't only have precompiles so one needs to make the distinction and take that case into account.

Also added async profiler support with a cli flag. All profilings are produced during execution (no warmup included) and each produce a profiling for the given test case. All options are passed directly to the profiler like so:
```
$ ethereum/evmtool/build/install/evmtool/bin/evmtool --fork Osaka benchmark --help
Usage: evm benchmark [-hV] [--[no]intrinsic-gas] [--json] [--json-alloc] [--[no]
                     memory] [--[no]native] [--[no]eip-3155] [--[no]time]
                     [--trace.[no]stack] [--trace.[no]returndata] [--trace.[no]
                     storage] [--[no]use-precompile-cache]
                     [--async-profiler=<asyncProfilerOptions>] [<benchmarks>...]
Execute an Ethereum State Test.
      [<benchmarks>...]     One or more of altBn128, EcRecover, ModExp,
                              Secp256k1, Bls12.
      --async-profiler=<asyncProfilerOptions>
                            Benchmark using async profiler. No profiler command
                              means profiling disabled. '%test-case' in the
                              file name expands to the test for which the
                              profiler ran.
```
```
$ ethereum/evmtool/build/install/evmtool/bin/evmtool --fork Osaka benchmark ModExp --async-profiler start,jfr,event=cpu,file=/tmp/%%test-case-%p.jfr
besu/v25.6-develop-8d6d455/osx-aarch_64/oracle_openjdk-java-21
Benchmarks for ModExp on fork Osaka
...
$ find /tmp/ -name *.jfr
/tmp/eip_example1-52856.jfr
/tmp/even-modulus-1-52856.jfr
/tmp/eip_example2-52856.jfr
```

body:
### Summary
This PR introduces support for Block-level Access List construction in line with [EIP-7928](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-7928.md). It adds a new serializable `BlockAccessList` data structure, along with a builder to record block-level account and storage accesses. Additionally, it introduces a `TransactionAccessList` used to track transaction-level accesses during execution.

During transaction processing, a `TransactionAccessList` is passed through the `MessageFrame` into the EVM, which records accessed accounts and storage keys. After processing, its contents are merged into the block-level `BlockAccessList`.

The `TransactionAccessList` only records touched accounts and slots, whereas the transaction-level updater is used to obtain the original and updated values. This approach allows correct read / write detection and handling of reverts.

The `BlockAccessList` is optionally included in the block body and its hash is stored in the block header under the `balHash` field. If present, the list is RLP-encoded.

BALs are constructed during:
* **Block processing** (in `AbstractBlockProcessor`) to validate `balHash` in the header and cache BALs for retrieval.
* **Block simulation** (in `BlockSimulator`) to include BALs in `eth_simulateV1` responses.
* **Block creation** (in `BlockCreator`) to embed BALs in newly built blocks.

### New feature flag
A new feature flag `--Xapi-block-access-list-enabled` enables:
* The `eth_getBlockAccessListByNumber` RPC endpoint.
* Caching BALs for the last `cache-last-blocks` imported blocks.
* Inclusion of BALs in block simulation outputs and call result objects.

### New RPC endpoint
`eth_getBlockAccessListByNumber` returns JSON-formatted block-level access list for a recently imported block from cache, identified by its block number or an alias like "latest", "finalized", "safe".

### New fork configuration
A new placeholder fork config "Amsterdam" (based on Osaka) has been introduced. When this fork is active:
* BALs are included in the block body and `balHash` in the block header of built blocks.
* During block import, if a BAL is present, its hash is compared against `balHash` in the header (mismatches are logged but do not invalidate blocks).
* BALs are always included in block simulation outputs regardless of the feature flag.

### Testing
The implementation has been testing by comparing `eth_simulateV1` outputs to those produced by [eth-bal-analysis](https://github.com/nerolation/eth-bal-analysis).

### TODO
- [x] Add transaction / block processing integration tests (possibly using [execution-spec-tests](https://github.com/ethereum/execution-spec-tests)), test BAL construction in `AbstractBlockProcessorIntegrationTest`.
- [x] Cleanups - pass the information whether BAL functionality was enabled via CLI or fork through `BlockAccessListManager` to avoid passing `isBlockAccessListEnabled` unnecessarily, ...
- [x] The output of `eth_getBlockAccessListByNumber` should include `balHash`
- [x] Make sure all tests pass.
- [ ] Add unit tests for newly added code (?)

Closes: #9080
title: EIP-7928 - Block-level Access Lists - construction

body:
## PR description
Address case where the pure java p256Verify implementation was not explicitly checking the public-key input is on-curve prior to verifying the signature.  This code path is not part of mainnet ethereum, but some specific configurations of besu on a dev net could have triggered this exception:
```
Invalid point coordinates
java.lang.IllegalArgumentException: Invalid point coordinates
	at org.bouncycastle.math.ec.ECCurve.validatePoint(Unknown Source)
	at org.bouncycastle.math.ec.ECCurve.decodePoint(Unknown Source)
	at org.hyperledger.besu.crypto.AbstractSECP256.verify(AbstractSECP256.java:373)
	at org.hyperledger.besu.crypto.SECP256R1.verifyMalleable(SECP256R1.java:132)
	at org.hyperledger.besu.evm.precompile.P256VerifyPrecompiledContract.computeDefault(P256VerifyPrecompiledContract.java:237)
	at org.hyperledger.besu.evm.precompile.P256VerifyPrecompiledContractTest.testInvalidCurvePointReturnsInvalid(P256VerifyPrecompiledContractTest.java:123)
	at java.base/jdk.internal.reflect.DirectMethodHandleAccessor.invoke(DirectMethodHandleAccessor.java:103)
	at java.base/java.lang.reflect.Method.invoke(Method.java:580)

```

Which would have caused these specifically configured instances of besu to ~~refuse to import or create a block with such an  invalid public key~~ rely on exceptional logic to attribute the precompile result as invalid, rather than proactively checking the public key is valid.

Thx @0xMushow for probing about point-validity in the besu implementation of eip-7951.  


## Fixed Issue(s)
<!-- Please link to fixed issue(s) here using format: fixes #<issue number> -->
<!-- Example: "fixes #2" -->
adds point-on-curve check in the pure java bouncy castle implementation, and adds unit tests to assert the behavior across pure java and native implementation.

body:
## PR description

Built on top of https://github.com/hyperledger/besu/pull/8736

Implement [EIP-7825: Transaction gas limit cap](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-7825)
The gas limit cap is driven by the protocol schedule, and at Osaka fork will be 30M, while before Osaka it is Long.MAX_VALUE that is a safe assumption, since no transactions have every or will reach that value before Osaka activation.
The 30M value is currently hard coded, but a future development can make it configurable, in case there is a need for that feature.

The transaction gas limit cap is also supported during simulations, with the following behavior:
- when estimating gas, the limit is enforced, following the fact that we already enforce the gas limit accordingly to the block gas limit or the sender balance
- instead for eth_call related calls, the limit is not enforced, since there it is already possible to specify a higher limit that the block gas limit, and the max depends on the [configuration option `rpc_gas_cap` ](https://besu.hyperledger.org/public-networks/reference/cli/options#rpc-gas-cap)

## Fixed Issue(s)

body:
## PR description

> We introduce an upper bound to the inputs of the precompile, each of the length inputs (length_of_BASE, length_of_EXPONENT and length_of_MODULUS) MUST be less than or equal to 8192 bits (1024 bytes).

> If any of these inputs are larger than the limit, the precompile execution stops, returns an error, and consumes all gas.

body:
## Description 

Fixes #8331, #8612

This PR implements EIP‑7594 “Peerdas changes” which update the handling of blob transactions and associated proofs across the protocol, transaction encoding/decoding, API endpoints, and internal data structures.

- Introduces support for multiple blob proof bundle versions (VERSION_0_KZG_PROOFS and VERSION_1_KZG_CELL_PROOFS) in protocol specifications and transaction processing.
- Updates encoding/decoding logic and builders to include a version ID, and adds new JSON‑RPC methods (e.g., `EngineGetPayloadV5` and `EngineGetBlobsV2`).
- Refactors internal data structures, replacing the BlobQuad with the new BlobProofBundle and adjusting tests accordingly.
- Adds a new `OsakaTransactionPoolPreProcessor` to upgrade locally submitted transactions with version 0 blob commitments to version 1 during pre-processing.

labels.name: Osaka

title: feat: implement EIP-7594 - PeerDAS

body:
## PR description
In the case where the system contract doesn't exist, Besu currently fails silently. The specs have been updated to say (withdrawal and consolidation requests only):

https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-2935 (Same as before)
> * if no code exists at HISTORY_STORAGE_ADDRESS, the call must fail silently,

https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-7002 (New behaviour)
> * If there is no code at `WITHDRAWAL_REQUEST_PREDEPLOY_ADDRESS`, the corresponding block **MUST** be marked invalid.

https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-7251 (New behaviour)
> * If there is no code at CONSOLIDATION_REQUEST_PREDEPLOY_ADDRESS, the corresponding block MUST be marked invalid.

## Fixed Issue(s)
fixes #8511

title: fix: fail system call if contract does not exist or code is empty

body:
<!-- Have you done the following? -->
<!--   * read the Code of Conduct? By filing an Issue, you are expected to -->  
<!--     comply with it, including treating everyone with respect: -->
<!--     https://github.com/hyperledger/besu/blob/main/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md -->
<!--   * Reproduced the issue in the latest version of the software -->
<!--   * Read the debugging docs: https://besu.hyperledger.org/private-networks/how-to -->
<!--   * Duplicate Issue check:  https://github.com/search?q=+is%3Aissue+repo%3Ahyperledger/Besu -->

### Overview

Hello developers.

I'm running a separate Pectra PoS testnet chain for API testing. During testing, I observed discrepancies in the JSON-RPC responses from execution clients—geth, besu, nethermind, and reth. Specifically, when calling `engine_getBlobsV1`, Besu returns an error with HTTP status code 400, while the other three clients respond successfully.

Thanks for your time!

### Steps to Reproduce

Call the `engine_getBlobsV1` with:

```shell
curl POST -v http://127.0.0.1:8551 \
   -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   -H "Authorization: Bearer your_encoded_jwt_token" \
   -d '{
   "id": 1,
   "jsonrpc": "2.0",
   "method": "engine_getBlobsV1",
   "params": [ ["0x000657f37554c781402a22917dee2f75def7ab966d7b770905398eba3c444014"] ]
 }'
```


**Expected behavior:** [What you expect to happen]

Return status code 200 with:
```json
{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 1, "result": [null]}
```
**Actual behavior:** [What actually happens]
Return 400 with:
```shell
*   Trying 127.0.0.1:8551...
* TCP_NODELAY set
* Connected to 127.0.0.1 (127.0.0.1) port 8551(#0)
> POST / HTTP/1.1
> Host: 127.0.0.1:8551
> User-Agent: curl/7.68.0
> Accept: */*
> Content-Type: application/json
> Authorization: Bearer your_encoded_jwt_token
> Content-Length: 154
> 
* upload completely sent off: 154 out of 154 bytes
* Mark bundle as not supporting multiuse
< HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request
< Content-Type: application/json
< transfer-encoding: chunked
< 
* Connection #0 to host 127.0.0.1 left intact
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"error":{"code":-32600,"message":"Invalid Request"}}
```
**Frequency:** [What percentage of the time does it occur?]

Always, on four kinds of besu-CL clients.

### Logs

Results from other EL clients.
```json
{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 1, "error": {"code": -32600, "message": "Invalid Request"}} # besu
{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 1, "result": [null]} # geth
{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 1, "result": [null]} # nethermind
{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 1, "result": [null]} # reth
```

### Versions
* OS Name & Version: Ubuntu-20.04

Client versions

- Besu/v25.4.1/linux-x86_64/openjdk-java-21
- Geth/v1.15.12-unstable-3e356d69-20250507/linux-amd64/go1.24.3
- Nethermind/v1.32.0-unstable+ff9befb5/linux-x64/dotnet9.0.4
- Reth/v1.3.12-6f8e725/x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu

with 

- Prysm/v6.0.0 (linux amd64)
- Lighthouse/v7.0.1-e42406d/x86_64-linux
- Nimbus/v25.4.1-77cfa7-stateofus
- Teku/v25.4.1/linux-x86_64/-eclipseadoptium-openjdk64bitservervm-java-21

labels.name: P3

title: `engine_getBlobsV1` Fails on Besu with 400 Error

body:
### Steps to Reproduce

Besu has not been reporting bad blocks, specifically invalid blocks produced by other members of a eof devnet environment.  For example on https://forkmon.eof-devnet-0.ethpandaops.io/ only geth and nethermind are reporting blocks they disagree with.


**Expected behavior:** Besu's bad blocks RPC returns bad blocks recently seen

**Actual behavior:** No bad blocks seen 

**Frequency:** whenever a blad block occurs

To reproduce run this Kurtosis environment:
```
participants_matrix:
  el:
    - el_type: besu
      el_image: ethpandaops/besu:main
    - el_type: nethermind
      el_image: ethpandaops/nethermind:feature-evm-eof-e1e375c
    # - el_type: reth
    #   el_image: ethpandaops/reth:main
    - el_type: geth
      el_image: ethpandaops/geth:osaka-mega-eof
  cl:
    - cl_type: prysm
      cl_image: ethpandaops/prysm-beacon-chain:develop-557c5be    
network_params:
  electra_fork_epoch: 1
  fulu_fork_epoch: 2
  seconds_per_slot: 4
additional_services:
  - dora
  - el_forkmon
  - assertoor
assertoor_params:
  image: "ethpandaops/assertoor:latest"
  tests:
    - file: https://gist.githubusercontent.com/shemnon/3854a75448dff222b04a09909ad1394c/raw/fulu-activated.yaml
    - file: https://gist.githubusercontent.com/shemnon/3854a75448dff222b04a09909ad1394c/raw/EOF-create-with-short-container.yaml
``` 

Using the forkmon we should start seeing a fork very early in epoch 2, with bad blocks reported by nethermind sometime before block 70.  Besu never reports Nethermind's produced blocks as bad, whereas Geth does.

To update besu you will need to deploy a local docker version and update the `el-image` tag.  The nethermind image is a version with a known bug that will produce bad blocks associated with the assertoor test.

### Logs
No relevant logs

### Versions (Add all that apply)
master

### Additional Information (Add any of the following or anything else that may be relevant)
Perhaps the fork choice rule is preventing the block from being considered?

labels.description: Something isn't working
labels.name: bug

title: Besu is not reporting bad blocks

body:
## PR description

Drive Cancun, Prague and Osaka target and max blobs per block from genesis config:
```
"blobSchedule": {
  "cancun": {
    "target": 3,
    "max": 6
   },
  "prague": {
    "target": 6,
    "max": 9
  },
  "osaka": {
    "target": 9,
    "max": 12
  }
}
```

If blobSchedule is missing, use the mainnet default values, i.e. same as config above.

Target is wired into appropriate *GasCalculator implementations
Max is wired into appropriate *TargetingGasLimitCalculator implementations

This PR does not remove EIP-7742 (although overlaps slightly)

This has been tested with a local pectra interop and successfully produced and validated blocks containing up to 9 blobs. 

`2024-12-19 02:42:22.551+00:00 | vert.x-worker-thread-0 | INFO  | AbstractEngineNewPayload | Imported #34  (8c3c2.....3c8e7)|   58 tx|  0 ws| 9 blobs| base fee  17.89 mwei| gas used   2,390,426 (  8.0%)| exec time 0.010s| mgas/s 239.04| peers:  2`

## PR description

## Fixed Issue(s)
